464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# SOP Audit: The Forge, Belfast

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member cancellation/win-back workflow** – who initiates contact, what objections trigger escalation, retention incentives, timing cadence
- **Complaint & incident logging** – from minor feedback to safeguarding concerns; escalation triggers, documentation standards, follow-up timelines
- **Coach performance management cycle** – feedback frequency, underperformance flags, improvement plans, exit protocols
- **Financial reconciliation & cash handling** – daily takings, PT package reconciliation, refund approval authority, audit trail
- **New member onboarding beyond signup** – first class experience, progress check-in at day 7/30, drop-off intervention points
- **Health & safety incident response** – injury reporting, near-miss logging, equipment failure protocol, insurance/GP notification
- **Class cancellation protocol** – when/how to notify members, substitute coach rules, refund/credit policy
- **Complaints handling formal process** – acknowledgment timeline, investigation steps, resolution communication, record retention
- **Staff HR & absence management** – sick leave, holiday approval, disciplinary triggers, exit interviews
- **Social media moderation & crisis response** – brand protection, negative review handling, COVID/injury-related posts

**Needs updating**

- **Marketing SOPs (2 of 12 missing)** – Email Newsletter and Blog Post are listed as gaps; unclear if **Social Media Content Calendar**, **Paid Ad Management**, **Referral Program Mechanics** exist or are stale
- **Coaches section** – likely missing: **Coach Illness/Absence Cover**, **Performance Review Cycle**, **Member Complaint About Coach** escalation
- **Client Onboarding** – 22 SOPs built but probably no **30-Day Retention Checkpoint** or **Long-Term Member Re-engagement** (

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Actions

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Pricing power exists — use it.** AR/M (Average Revenue per Member) has collapsed from £171.81 (April post-move) to £120.48 (June). You've added 39 members (+36%) but revenue per head fell 30%. Audit membership tiers immediately: introduce a premium tier (personal training add-on, peak-hours-only tier, or class packages) to lift blended rate back to £160+ within 60 days. Test a £5–10/month increase on new sign-ups.

• **Attrition spiked post-move (8.33% April, 5.74% May) but June hit 0% — hold that.** Don't lose momentum. June's 147 active clients is your strongest base. Push a 3-month "Refer a Friend" campaign (£20 credit per successful referral) to convert that zero-churn goodwill into growth; target 15 new members by end of July.

• **Non-peak capacity is empty.** With a studio now "twice the size," morning (6–9am) and off-peak afternoon (2–4pm) classes are likely under-utilised. Run a "Daytime Hero" discount (£15/month add-on for daytime-only access) or corporate partnerships (local offices, 10× £12/month contracts = £120/month recurring). Even 8–10 off-peak-only members = +£1,200/year margin.

---

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-People expenses are the problem — they've tripled since pre-move.** May–June average £9,312/month vs. Sept–Nov average £4,574/month. June's £9,390 is unsustainable at £17,710 revenue (53% of top line). Breakdown required immediately: facility lease/utilities, equipment maintenance, insurance, supplies. If lease jumped post-relocation, that's explainable; if it's supplies/services bloat, negotiate supplier contracts and cut waste. Target: £6,500/month (37% of revenue) by August.

• **People costs are controlled and appropriate.** June payroll £3,686 on £20,198 revenue (18%) is healthy and improving trend. Staffing headcount is right-sized for your 147-member base. Keep this lever stable.

---

## IMPROVE PROFITABILITY & TAX

• **Profit margin collapsed to 5.28% (June) after a strong May (29.29%).** The non-people expense spike is

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Reassign Grace's classes immediately:** Her 17 Build: Lower and 9 Build: Upper sessions should transfer to JoJo (who already leads these) and Eilis (to develop her range). This removes 26 coaching hours from Grace in one move.

• **Move Elevate and Strongevity off Grace:** JoJo takes Elevate (she runs other format classes), Eilis takes Strongevity to stretch her skills on a lower-demand day. Covers 9 sessions and further reduces Grace to admin/operations only.

• **Redistribute Grace's No Sweat Intros to Eilis or JoJo:** Three intro sessions are low-risk; Eilis running these builds confidence and member relationships without technical demand.

• **JoJo is carrying 44 PTs — unsustainable long-term:** Eilis has only 11. Actively move 8–10 of JoJo's PT clients to Eilis over the next cycle (stagger handovers to keep continuity), freeing JoJo to coach more group classes and reduce her solo dependency.

• **Body Composition scans (InBody) don't count as coaching load:** Delegate all 20 of Grace's scans and 11 of JoJo's to Eilis as admin tasks; this frees real coaching time without skill risk and trains Eilis on member data.

• **Tuesday coverage stays with JoJo; use Eilis on low-skill Monday/Wed/Thu slots** to balance her week and prevent JoJo carrying all technically complex sessions.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio gaps:**

1. **Launch a "Build Accessory" open-gym block** on Thursday and Monday mornings (your quietest days) between PT slots — members doing Build classes often want extra arm/core work, so this fills natural demand without cannibalizing class bookings.

2. **Run a paid "Strength Foundations" workshop** Saturday mornings (lowest attendance day) — 45 mins for 6-8 members on technique for deadlifts/squats; price as premium add-on; targets newer members who might fear the Build classes.

**For quieter classes:**

3. **Move Engine to Tuesday or Wednesday at 6:30am** (test 2 weeks) — it's a cardio/conditioning class competing against strength on quiet days; your busiest days have proven traffic, and early slots often suit commuters; pair with a targeted email: "New time: Engine now Tuesday mornings—hit conditioning before work."

4. **Rebrand Capacity as "Metabolic Finisher"** and position it as a 20-min add-on *after* Build classes (same studio, back-to-back slots) — removes it from competing as standalone; members already warmed up; frames it as intensity booster rather than standalone commitment.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Build — Tactical Execution for The Forge

• **Start with your revenue reality, not your wishlist.** Map what coaches *actually* deliver daily (group classes, 1-1 sessions, nutrition checks, form reviews) and what members *actually* pay for now. Build the menu from observed behaviour, not aspirational offerings. This kills the "we offer everything" trap.

• **Create two separate docs immediately.** Coach version = what they're authorized to deliver, pricing caps, upsell rules (prevents inconsistent promises). Member version = benefit-focused copy ("get form feedback" not "biomechanical assessment"). Test the member version with 5 current members for comprehension.

• **Price it anchored to local competition + your positioning.** Get 3 other Belfast gyms' rates, 3 premium studios in UK (as ceiling). Price group classes at a point that makes 1-1 sessions feel like obvious upgrades. Build a simple spreadsheet: service | cost to deliver (coach time + facility) | margin target (usually 60%+).

• **Make it digital and static simultaneously.** One clean one-page PDF (email/print), plus a Google Sheet coaches can pull from. Hard-to-update menus die. Link the sheet to your booking system so pricing stays live.

• **Have Grace personally validate pricing with 3 high-paying members this week.** Not a survey—a coffee chat. Ask: "Would you pay £X for Y?" Watch their face. Adjust before launch.

What's your current pricing spread like across services?

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

Your CPL has inflated 4.5x in four months (£6 to £27) whilst spend has collapsed 75% (£435 to £109). This isn't budget cuts working—it's audience fatigue and creative decay. July's 4 leads on £109 suggests your ads have gone stale: Meta's algorithm is serving to the same exhausted pool repeatedly, driving cost up and volume down. The close rate holding at 75% shows your offer still converts, so the problem is reaching *new* prospects, not the messaging itself.

The April baseline (69 leads, £6/lead) proves The Forge can acquire cost-effectively—you've simply stopped doing what worked then. You're now in a doom loop: lower budget → fewer impressions → weaker learning data for Meta's algorithm → worse targeting efficiency → higher CPL → temptation to cut budget further.

**Actions**

1. **Immediately rebuild budget to £400–500/month and segment your audience stack.** You've likely been retargeting the same warm audience for months. Split into three ad sets: (a) cold lookalike from your best customers (post-sale data), (b) interest-based targeting (women 25–45, fitness + wellness interests), (c) geographic—focus hard on Belfast postcodes where footfall converts best. Starve the stale audience; feed fresh ones.

2. **Refresh creative ruthlessly.** April's winning ads are now invisible to Meta's algorithm and viewer eyes. Test three new angles: (i) "6 weeks to confident" (emotional/transformation angle vs. fitness-focused), (ii) user-generated content from recent trial members (social proof), (iii) a short-form video of actual class footage (removes mystery, lowers trial anxiety). Pause anything running for >4 weeks.

3. **Test a lower-friction lead magnet.** Your current 6-week trial offer is strong but high-commitment. Run a parallel campaign: "Free nutrition guide + gym tour" at £0 lead-gen to build a warm pipeline you can nurture into trials at lower CAC. This gives Meta fresh conversion events to optimise on.

4. **Single biggest lever: reallocate £150/week to

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF

## REVENUE: ARREST THE DECLINE & FIX CHURN

• **Rev/member is sliding (£138 in April → £107 in June; 3-month avg £120 vs prior 3-month avg £128).** You're adding members (+27 from Feb to June) but revenue per head is falling 6%—this is upsell and pricing leakage, not headcount. Action: audit June cohort—are new members on lower tier plans? Review class card and PT upsell conversion. If >20% of new joiners skip paid add-ons, fix onboarding sales process immediately.

• **June churn 3.2% looks good, but May's 11.9% was severe.** That's 16–17 members lost in one month. Action: run exit surveys from May leavers (likely post-Easter, post-relocation friction). Offer 4-week winback at 20% discount to May/June churners; track reactivation rate. If May was noise, proceed; if it recurs, retention bonus (class pack or PT credit) for tenure >6 months becomes non-negotiable.

• **New revenue lines:** You're at 148 members in June on memberships alone. Launch: (1) class packages (10-class card at £85, upsell from unlimited at £35/month); (2) small-group PT (pairs/trios at £25/head/session, fill quiet slots 10am–4pm weekdays). Both leverages existing space post-relocation, zero marginal cost. Target: +£800/month by August.

---

## EXPENSES: SPOTLIGHT MARCH & APRIL CASH BURNS

• **Cash out spiked March (£23k), April (£39k). March was +£8k vs Feb spend; April was relocation. Strip April, and March remains 41% above Feb (£23k vs £16.5k).** What drove it? Stock, rent double-payment, equipment, or payroll overage? Action: itemize March outflows from Starling—if it's repeatable payroll/supplier cost, cut it now; if one-off, confirm it's gone. If it returns, you're at breakeven despite 148 members, which signals cost structure is misaligned.

• **May–June cash out stabilized (£16.6k, £20.6k) post-relocation; that's healthy.** Staffing, rent, utilities within expected range. Monitor June at £20.6k against your 148-member baseline—if you're still ov
